3A_Bibliographic.citation: Nagata, J. M., Cortez, C. A., Cattle, C. J., Ganson, K. T., Iyer, P., Bibbins-Domingo, K., & Baker, F. C. (2022). Parent-Adolescent Discrepancies in Adolescent Recreational Screen Time Reporting During the Coronavirus Disease 2019 Pandemic. Academic Pediatrics, 22(5), 831-838.
3A_Bibliographic.notes: Author list inferred from DOI metadata.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We analyzed data from Survey 1 collected from May 16 to 22, 2020. Participants who completed the COVID-19 ABCD surveys out of order, and participants with missing recreational screen time, sociodemographic, and/or incomplete parent/adolescent dyad data, were excluded from the analysis, resulting in an analytic sample of 5335 participants.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Sociodemographic characteristics (age, sex, race/ethnicity, country of birth, primary language, parent marital status, parent education, household income)
3C_Exposure.modalities: N/A - this is a measurement concordance study; exposure is sociodemographics, outcome is screen time reporting discrepancy
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Categorical and continuous sociodemographic variables as predictors of parent-adolescent screen time report discrepancies
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Sociodemographic characteristics were based on parent report and included adolescent characteristics such as age (continuous), sex (male or female), race/ethnicity (White, Latinx/Hispanic, Black, Asian, Native American, other), primary language (English or other), and country of birth (United States or outside United States).
3C_Exposure.notes: This paper examines measurement concordance of screen time reporting between parents and adolescents. It does not examine screen time as an exposure predicting a health outcome.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Absolute difference in parent- vs. adolescent-reported recreational screen time (hours/day)
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: ABCD COVID-19 Survey recreational screen time questions (parallel parent and adolescent versions)
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Adolescents and parents reported time spent in a typical day for the adolescent using screens in a recreational capacity... Differences in parent- and adolescent-reported recreational screen time were determined by subtracting adolescent-reported recreational screen time hours from parent-reported recreational screen time hours.
3D_Outcome.notes: The outcome is the discrepancy between parent and adolescent screen time reports, not a health/behavioral outcome of screen time.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Paired t-tests for parent-adolescent dyad comparisons; intraclass correlation coefficients (ICC) for concordance; linear regression for sociodemographic predictors of absolute screen time reporting discrepancies; ANOVA for group comparisons; propensity weights applied
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, race/ethnicity, country of birth, primary language, parent marital status, parent education, household income
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Linear regression analyses assessed whether sociodemographic characteristics (adolescent age, sex, race, country of birth, primary language, parent marital status, parent education, and household income) were associated with absolute differences in parent-adolescent-reported recreational screen time across screen time categories. All data analyses were conducted in 2021 using Stata 15.1... utilized a statistical significance threshold of P = .05, and applied propensity weights to match key sociodemographic variables in ABCD Study to the American Community Survey by the US Census.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Mean difference in hours/day (parent minus adolescent); paired t-test t=10.58; ICC=0.43

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Absolute difference in total recreational screen time reporting
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Hours/day difference per year of age

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Absolute difference in total recreational screen time reporting
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Hours/day difference; Black vs White reference

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Absolute difference in total recreational screen time reporting
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Hours/day difference; Latino/Hispanic vs White reference

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Absolute difference in total recreational screen time reporting

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Mean difference hours/day; adolescents reported more; ICC=0.51
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Multiplayer gaming: Difference -0.08 (-0.15, -0.02), t=-2.59, P=.010, ICC 0.51

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Mean difference hours/day; adolescents reported more; ICC=0.34
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Social media: Difference -0.09 (-0.14, -0.03), t=-3.02, P=.003, ICC 0.34

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Mean difference hours/day; parents reported more; ICC=0.29
Mean difference hours/day; parents reported more; ICC=0.32
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Video chatting: Difference 0.31 (0.26, 0.35), t=13.49, P<.001, ICC 0.32

3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: This is a measurement concordance study, not a screen-time-to-health-outcome study. Effect size evaluation is not directly applicable in the same way. The paper reports exact p-values for some tests and uses < thresholds for others.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: pediatricians could consider assessing screen usage in primary care visits and discussing potential risks and benefits. The American Academy of Pediatrics endorses a Family Media Use Plan which encourages parents to develop a personalized plan with their children.
3H_Recommendations.notes: Recommendations focus on improving parent-adolescent communication about screen time, not on reducing screen time based on health outcome evidence from this study.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion interprets findings as demonstrating that parents and adolescents disagree on screen time estimates, with parents generally reporting higher total screen time but adolescents reporting more gaming and social media. Racial/ethnic and socioeconomic disparities in reporting discrepancies are attributed to differential monitoring capacity (e.g., essential worker status of Black and Latino parents). The paper recommends family media plans and better measurement tools rather than screen time reduction.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: This is a measurement/methods paper about screen time reporting concordance, not a screen-time-to-health-outcome study. It may not be directly relevant to a systematic review of screen time effects on health outcomes.
